item.title: You could detailed the Bear Inccident lol
item.content:encoded: I guess it would be fun

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Doesn't the first sentence in Special Containment Procedures seem out of place? I thought the general style was to describe it without disclosing specifics about the object first.

This article was originally posted in 2008, before there was a standardized formatting. As such, it doesn't quite adhere to current standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Doesn't the first sentence in Special Containment Procedures seem out of place? I thought the general style was to describe it without disclosing specifics about the object first. IMO it could be very well moved down to the description still reading well and be more in line with the SCP style.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to see that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No it would not.

The Earth has survived volcanic eruptions in the tens of thousands (or even hundreds of thousands) of megatons before (such as the Mount Tambora eruption in 1815, at an estimated 33,000 MT), and at least one asteroid impact on the order of a hundred million megatons.

If you're looking to literally blow up the entire planet, you'll need somewhere in the neighborhood of 43 quadrillion megatons, although you'll certainly start causing significant problems for life on the surface well before then.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to think that that part was a prank by the O5 Council on the Administrator.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: hmmm… now to think of something involving 682 with this…

item.title: Mod Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Upshot-Knothole_GRABLE.jpg

Image Source, Public Domain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is the Administrator still whining about his pickup used for a goddamn gigantic howitzer?

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Noting that a recent edit by DrHelzPolowski was reverted, as it introduced new content beyond a spelling or grammar fix. The Site Rules indicate that such an edit is not allowed to be made without permission from the original author or staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And so to this date the administrator is still pissed about their pickup truck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That poor administrator. I hope they gave him a new pickup.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: rate of speed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When I read this, the details weren't listed. I'm almost wondering if it could be an -EX

http://mentalfloss.com/uk/technology/27079/the-nuclear-warhead-that-needed-a-chicken-in-its-casing

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Issat a nuke cannon

Dassa nuke cannon

+1+1+1+1+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd love to think some trigger happy Foundation researcher in range of the firing mechanism caught sight of a bear mauling an agent and in his panicked fired at it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In regards to the picture, I've found a neat little video

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to edit this slightly because there are about three instances of scp-044(one(1) being in Aberdeen, Maryland) and they were transported by two trucks(one(1) on each end) not by railroad.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: actually its from the grable-ford nuclear test of operation upshot knot-hole, my dad got me a movie about nuclear tests when I was little

item.title: what about density
item.content:encoded:
does density of material matter?
Also, what about SCP-115?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is a cool picture the only thing that bothers me is that UPshot-knothole Grabble was in 1953 and the M65 was developed in the early 1950s after WW2. But overall it's very well written.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't really get past the fact that the cannon in the image is clearly an M65 Atomic Cannon and that the image in question is the fairly well known Upshot-Knothole Grable test picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A giant cannon that can make a giant explosion from any form of ammo?

Hmm, why does it being Safe feel odd….?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I went and did the math on this one, if it was 100% conversion of the diesel truck, the energy output would be equivalent of a 19,118 Megaton bomb. Correct me if I'm wrong but wouldn't that blow up most of… well Earth?

Either way, I upvoted. Nice concept there author.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second this motion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a good article, but the image absolutely ruins it for me. I saw the exact same image in my Sophomore history textbook in the chapter about the start of the Cold War.

Sorry, but no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a great article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You mean you liked it better when it was a thing that didn't exist yet?

Seriously, check out the histories of the relevant pages, you will find that 044 was created earlier than 2041-J. A year and four months earlier, to be precise.

Now, on to other things:

What was the "Bear Incident"? It intrigues me, and I wish to learn more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this better when it was called SCP-2041-J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know mixing SCPs is generally a no-no, but this thing could make quick work of some Keter-class objects: fire something organic into http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/scp-804 perhaps?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, because it's still driving me nuts, and I'm a stickler for accuracy in this kind of thing, can I correct the image caption? Please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That image of the american made atomic cannon is fairly well known. You may wish to use an image of one of Hitler's actual giant cannons, Such as
http://www.avalanchepress.com/images/2008/dora_04.jpg
or
http://4.bp.blogspot.com/_RXKVjtRHGDY/S7rBPj-R-6I/AAAAAAAAD_0/Yss9amrEQ6Q/s1600/Sans+titre-Num__risation-01.jpeg
or
http://www.3ad.com/history/wwll/feature.pages/eick.pages/eick.photos/giant.cannon.jpg

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The test name (of the image) was Upshot-Knothole Grable. As far as I know, there's not a Ford Grable. The reason it was chosen in reality was because it was phonetic for G (gun, as it was the M65 nuclear artillery cannon). Before the NATO phonetic alphabet, mind you, so nobody needs to jump in and tell me that the phonetic for G is Golf now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What did you think is misspelled? Everything looks right except "Knolte Ford Grable", which I read as mission-title nonsense plus the projectile's truck's model name. If there's no such thing as a Ford Grable, then I stand corrected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any particular reason the picture caption is misspelled? OR am I missing something here?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Excellent, then. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: [brushes through mental cobwebs] It's not an extra box. There could be a decimal point in there. Or not. Yay for ambiguity!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The number for the mass-to-energy conversion yield is blacked out, yes, but it's three digits. Either it's 100%, or this thing is patently impossible. Is there an extra █ in there, or did you mean to imply extraphysical mechanisms? (Either way is good, heh.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had changed this from Euclid to Safe several weeks ago, under the "guns are safe" rationale. After quite a bit of thought, I decided to leave it as is for the Mass Edit, mainly because (a) I did announce the class change at the time (and no one objected), and (b) the addendum still amuses me. If it does get changed back to Euclid, I'd suggest adding a reason why it can't be left as Safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted. Cool idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to see more on the history of this object. Where did we find it? Was it used in the war?

item.title: A Nuke Gun?
item.content:encoded:
That's a cool idea- a relatively portable nuclear cannon. Just put some legs, backup weapons and a cockpit on it and presto! You've made a Metal Gear!

Or you could just combine it with SCP-244…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with snorlison. It is destructive as hell, but isn't going to do anything unless used. I'd say Euclid is sufficient, even if it is a hell of a destructive Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd suggest that this isn't Keter. Dangerous yes, with the utmost priority to contain. But it's ultimately containable, able to be disassembled, etc.

I suggest this be upgraded to Euclid and the Keter classification dropped.
